payload.formula_text:
ROAS = Revenue / Spend
CPA = Spend / Conversions
CVR = (Conversions / Clicks) Ã— 100
payload.formula_explanation: Compares performance metrics across audience categories (Retargeting, Lookalike, CRM/Email, Prospecting) and platforms (Google Ads, Meta Ads). Each bar represents the aggregated performance of all audiences within that category.
how_to_read: Grouped bars show impressions by audience category and platform
Taller bars indicate higher impression volume for that category
Compare Google (blue) vs Meta (purple) performance within each category
Retargeting typically shows highest efficiency, Prospecting typically shows lowest
Use the legend to understand which platform each color represents
decisions_answered: Which audience type delivers the best performance across platforms?
How does Google Ads audience targeting compare to Meta Ads?
Should we invest more in retargeting vs prospecting?
Which platform should receive more budget for each audience type?
payload.narration_text:
This chart compares audience performance across four key categories: Retargeting, Lookalike, CRM/Email, and Prospecting.

The grouped bars show impressions by category, with separate colors for Google Ads and Meta Ads.

Retargeting audiences typically perform best because they target users who already know your brand. Lookalike audiences extend your reach to similar users. CRM/Email audiences are your existing customers, and Prospecting reaches entirely new users.

Use this to decide: Which audience type should get more budget? Which platform performs better for each type? And is your current allocation aligned with performance?

payload.formula_text:
ROAS = Revenue / Cost
CTR = (Clicks / Impressions) Ã— 100
CPA = Cost / Conversions
payload.formula_explanation: Shows performance for Google Ads custom audiences including AUTO (Google-generated), PURCHASE_INTENT, and SEARCH custom audiences. These are keyword-based and interest-based targeting options.
how_to_read: AUTO audiences are Google-generated based on your business signals
PURCHASE_INTENT audiences target users actively researching purchases
SEARCH audiences are based on custom search terms you define
Days active shows how long each audience has been running
Compare performance across audience types to optimize targeting strategy
decisions_answered: Which custom audience type performs best for my business?
Are Google auto-generated audiences effective?
Which search custom audiences should I expand?
Should I create more purchase intent audiences?
payload.narration_text:
Custom audiences are keyword and interest-based targeting options in Google Ads.

There are three main types: AUTO audiences that Google generates automatically, PURCHASE_INTENT audiences targeting users researching purchases, and SEARCH audiences based on custom search terms you define.

This table helps you compare performance across these types. Look at the ROAS column to see which audiences drive profitable returns.

Purchase intent audiences often perform well because they target users with clear buying signals. Search custom audiences let you define exactly what topics to target.

Use this data to decide which custom audience types deserve more investment and which need refinement.

payload.formula_text:
Revenue per User = Total Revenue / Active Users
Conversion Rate = (Transactions / Sessions) Ã— 100
payload.formula_explanation: Shows performance of GA4 audiences including predictive audiences like "Likely 7-day purchasers" and "Likely churners". These audiences use machine learning to predict user behavior.
how_to_read: Purchasers audience shows your actual buying customers for comparison
Likely 7-day purchasers are predicted to buy soon - high priority targets
Likely churning purchasers are at risk of not returning
Revenue per user shows customer value by audience
Session duration and page views indicate engagement levels
decisions_answered: How accurate are GA4 predictive audiences?
Which predictive audience should I target for remarketing?
What is the revenue potential of likely purchasers?
How large is my at-risk customer segment?
payload.narration_text:
GA4 predictive audiences use machine learning to identify users likely to take specific actions.

The Purchasers row shows actual buyers - your baseline for comparison. Likely 7-day purchasers are predicted to buy soon - these are high-value remarketing targets.

Likely churning purchasers are at risk of not returning - consider retention campaigns for this segment.

Revenue per user helps compare value across audiences. Non-purchasers typically show near-zero revenue, while purchasers and likely purchasers show much higher values.

Use these audiences to build more targeted campaigns. Export likely purchasers to Google Ads for high-value remarketing. Target likely churners with retention offers.

payload.formula_text:
Member Count = COUNT(DISTINCT PROFILE_ID) per LIST
Type = LIST or SEGMENT
payload.formula_explanation: Shows Klaviyo email list and segment membership counts. These are your owned audiences â€” subscribers who opted in to receive email marketing. List size directly impacts email channel reach and revenue potential.
how_to_read: Lists are sorted by member count (largest first)
Type column distinguishes static Lists from dynamic Segments
Member count shows active subscribers in each list
Compare list sizes to understand audience distribution
Use search to find specific lists by name
decisions_answered: How large are our email marketing audiences?
Which lists have the most subscribers?
How are subscribers distributed across lists?
Which lists should we prioritize for campaigns?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows your Klaviyo email lists and segments with subscriber counts.

These are your owned audiences â€” people who opted in to receive your email marketing. Unlike paid ad audiences, these are free to reach.

Lists are sorted by member count so your largest audiences appear first. The type column shows whether it's a static List or a dynamic Segment.

Use this to understand your email reach potential and identify which lists to target for campaigns. Larger lists offer more reach, but engagement rates matter too â€” cross-reference with the Creative Performance email table.

payload.formula_text:
ROAS = Revenue / Cost
Cell Value = Selected Metric (ROAS, CPA, CTR, or Conversions)
payload.formula_explanation: Heatmap showing audience performance across different ad networks (YouTube, Search, Display, etc.) and devices (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet). Darker colors indicate better performance.
how_to_read: Rows represent different ad networks (YouTube, Search, Display, etc.)
Columns represent device types (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet)
Darker green cells indicate higher ROAS - better performance
Lighter or yellow cells indicate lower performance
Cell size corresponds to relative spend level
Click any cell to filter page by that network/device combination
decisions_answered: Which network/device combination has the best ROAS?
Should I allocate more budget to mobile or desktop?
Is YouTube performing better on certain devices?
Where should I reduce or increase targeting?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap shows how audience targeting performs across different ad networks and devices.

Networks are on the Y-axis: YouTube, Search, Display, Discovery, and others. Devices are on the X-axis: Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, and Connected TV.

Darker green cells indicate higher ROAS - these are your best performing combinations. Lighter colors or reds indicate opportunities for optimization.

For example, if YouTube on Mobile shows 4x ROAS but only gets 10% of budget, consider shifting more spend there. If Search on Desktop shows 1.5x ROAS, consider reducing allocation.

Use this to guide both network selection and device bid adjustments in your campaigns.

payload.formula_text:
Frequency = Impressions / Reach
CTR = (Clicks / Impressions) Ã— 100
CPM Reach = (Cost / Reach) Ã— 1000
payload.formula_explanation: Scatter plot showing the relationship between average frequency (how often users see ads) and CTR (engagement rate) for Meta ad sets. Bubble size indicates spend level, color indicates efficiency zone.
how_to_read: X-axis shows average frequency (times each user sees your ad)
Y-axis shows CTR - higher means better engagement at that frequency
Green bubbles (1-2x frequency) are in the optimal efficiency zone
Yellow bubbles (2-3.5x frequency) are approaching saturation
Red bubbles (3.5x+ frequency) show audience saturation - consider refresh
Larger bubbles indicate higher spend - prioritize optimizing these
decisions_answered: Which ad sets are showing audience saturation (too high frequency)?
What is the optimal frequency before CTR starts declining?
Which high-spend ad sets need creative refresh?
Should I expand reach or accept higher frequency?
payload.narration_text:
This scatter plot reveals the relationship between frequency and engagement for your Meta ad sets.

Frequency is how many times each user sees your ads. CTR measures how often they click. The goal is finding the sweet spot - enough frequency for message retention, but not so much that users tune out.

Green bubbles show ad sets in the optimal 1-2x frequency zone. Yellow indicates warning at 2 to 3.5x. Red shows saturation above 3.5x where performance typically drops.

Bubble size represents spend - bigger bubbles are your biggest investments, so prioritize those.

When you see red bubbles, consider refreshing creative, expanding the audience, or reducing bid caps to lower frequency.

payload.formula_text:
CTR by Frequency = Clicks at Frequency Band / Impressions at Frequency Band Ã— 100
CPC by Frequency = Cost at Frequency Band / Clicks at Frequency Band
payload.formula_explanation: Line chart showing how CTR and CPC change as frequency increases. Demonstrates diminishing returns - at higher frequencies, CTR drops and CPC rises, indicating audience fatigue.
how_to_read: X-axis shows frequency bands from 1.0 to 6.0+
Blue line shows CTR - typically highest at lower frequencies
Red dashed line shows CPC - typically rises with frequency
The optimal zone (usually 1.5-2.5x) balances reach and efficiency
Sharp CTR drops indicate the saturation point for your audiences
Use this to set frequency caps in your campaigns
decisions_answered: At what frequency do diminishing returns begin?
What frequency cap should I set for my campaigns?
How much does efficiency drop at high frequencies?
Is it worth the higher CPC for additional frequency?
payload.narration_text:
Saturation curves reveal how performance changes as users see your ads more frequently.

The blue CTR line typically starts high and drops as frequency increases - users get tired of seeing the same ads. The red CPC line usually rises because you're paying more for less engaged users.

The optimal zone is usually 1.5 to 2.5x frequency. Above this, you see diminishing returns.

This chart answers a critical question: at what point should I cap frequency? Find where CTR drops sharply - that's your saturation threshold.

Set your frequency caps just below this point to maintain efficiency while still reaching users enough times to drive action.

payload.formula_text:
ROAS = Revenue / Cost
CTR = (Clicks / Impressions) Ã— 100
CPA = Cost / Conversions
payload.formula_explanation: Displays performance metrics for each Google Ads remarketing user list. Lists are categorized by type (REMARKETING, CRM_BASED, SIMILAR, etc.) and sorted by revenue to highlight top performers.
how_to_read: Rows are sorted by revenue by default - top performers appear first
ROAS column shows return on ad spend with color coding (green > 3.0x, yellow 2-3x, red < 2x)
Audience size indicates potential reach for each list
Type badges show the list category (remarketing, CRM-based, lookalike, etc.)
Use search to find specific lists, sort by any column to reorder
decisions_answered: Which remarketing lists generate the most revenue?
Are my CRM-based lists outperforming website remarketing?
Which lists have high CTR but low conversions (consideration stage)?
Should I increase bids on high-performing lists?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows performance for all your Google Ads remarketing lists.

The key metrics are impressions, clicks, conversions, revenue, cost, CTR, and ROAS. Lists are sorted by revenue so your top performers appear first.

The type column tells you what kind of list it is - REMARKETING for website visitors, CRM_BASED for customer uploads, SIMILAR for lookalikes, and RULE_BASED for custom definitions.

Color coding helps interpretation: Green ROAS means strong returns above 3x, yellow is acceptable at 2-3x, and red below 2x needs attention.

Use this to identify which lists deserve more budget and which need optimization or retirement.
